did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning ensemble models (AdaBoost, CatBoost, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and Extra Trees) combined with a Max Voting method and a Genetic Algorithm (GA) for feature extraction to perform multi-class skin cancer classification using the ISIC 2018 and HAM10000 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a novel Max Voting ensemble approach for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods (AdaBoost, CatBoost, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, Extra Trees, Genetic Algorithm), datasets used (ISIC 2018, HAM10000), and reports new quantitative results (e.g., 95.80% and 96.20% accuracy). Although it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is to report new technical findings rather than summarize prior literature.

title: Advancing Skin Cancer Prediction Using Ensemble Models
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of multi-class skin cancer classification by proposing a Max Voting ensemble method to improve diagnostic accuracy and robustness. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 and ISIC 2018 datasets, applying a Genetic Algorithm for optimal feature extraction from dermoscopic images. Five ensemble models—AdaBoost, CatBoost, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and Extra Trees—were trained and combined using the Max Voting technique. Evaluation metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score demonstrated that the Max Voting approach significantly outperformed individual models. Specifically, the method achieved an accuracy of 95.80% on the HAM10000 dataset and 96.20% on the ISIC 2018 dataset. The results indicate that this ensemble strategy provides a more reliable and precise tool for distinguishing between various skin lesion types compared to standalone machine learning models.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin cancer lesion types (7 classes: actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, melanocytic nevi, melanoma, vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin cancer lesions using ensemble models (AdaBoost, CatBoost, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, Extra Trees) combined with a Max Voting method. The task involves distinguishing between seven discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions using the HAM10000 and ISIC 2018 datasets. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: Human Against Machine with 10,000 skin cancer lesion images. A public dataset containing dermatoscopic images of seven classes of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training ensemble models (AdaBoost, CatBoost, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, Extra Trees) after data augmentation balanced the classes to approximately 3200 images per class.
use.validation: Used for validation via 10-fold cross-validation to evaluate feature extraction and model performance.
use.testing: Used for testing the Max Voting ensemble model, achieving 95.80% accuracy.
use.other: Data augmentation was applied to balance the class distribution.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: 2018 International Skin Imaging Collaboration dataset. A public dataset containing dermoscopy images of seven distinct skin lesion types.

use.training: Used for training ensemble models (Random Forest, AdaBoost, CatBoost, Gradient Boosting, Extra Trees) for 32 epochs.
use.validation: Used for testing/evaluation with a specific validation subset of 1500 images.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the Max Voting ensemble model, achieving 96.20% accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the Max Voting ensemble method with optimized feature sets (61 features) on the validation dataset.
To analyze classification performance, visualize misclassification patterns, and differentiate between accurate and inaccurate predictions for seven classes.
To optimize feature extraction and determine the ideal feature set size (61 features) by maximizing information gain from color and texture features.
To combine predictions from five individual ensemble models (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, AdaBoost, CatBoost, Extra Trees) to determine the final class prediction.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

validation_dataset.description: Validation dataset with 1293 images after data augmentation balancing classes to ~3200 per class.
validation_dataset.notes: Max Voting method outperformed individual models. Class 5 had the highest precision, Class 1 had the lowest precision for Max Voting.

feature_optimization_results.description: Results from 10-fold cross-validation on validation set using optimal 61 features (23 color, 38 texture) selected by Genetic Algorithm.

validation_dataset.description: Validation dataset with 1500 images. Models trained for 32 epochs.
validation_dataset.notes: Max Voting produced the most remarkable overall metrics. It showed improved recall and accuracy for melanoma and melanocytic nevi.

comparison_with_state_of_the_art.description: Comparison of proposed Max Voting model accuracy against other recent studies.
comparison_with_state_of_the_art.notes: The proposed Max Voting model achieved the highest accuracy among the compared models.

architecture_used.main_model: Max Voting Ensemble of five pre-trained ensemble models: Random Forest (RF), Gradient Boosting (GB), AdaBoost (AB), CatBoost (CB), and Extra Trees (ET).
architecture_used.pretraining: The individual ensemble models (RF, GB, AB, CB, ET) are described as 'pre-trained' in the context of being loaded and used for prediction, but no specific pre-training datasets or weights are reported for the ensemble components themselves. The feature extraction relies on a Genetic Algorithm (GA) optimizing color and texture features.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported as image dimensions. Input consists of optimized feature vectors: 61 features total (23 color features and 38 texture features) extracted from dermoscopic images.

classification_or_output_head.description: Aggregates predictions from all five models. The class receiving the most votes is selected as the final prediction. Tie-breaking techniques include weighted voting based on model confidence or prioritizing the more accurate model.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a consistent dimension of 256 × 256 pixels
Edge detection using Canny edge detector
Data augmentation (rotation, scaling, flipping, cropping) to balance class distribution

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation, scaling, flipping, and cropping were applied to balance class distribution, increasing the frequency of underrepresented classes to approximately 3200 images per class.

conclusions: The proposed Max Voting ensemble method, which combines AdaBoost, CatBoost, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and Extra Trees, significantly improves predictive performance and robustness for multi-class skin cancer classification compared to individual ensemble models.

The Max Voting approach achieved an accuracy of 95.80% on the HAM10000 dataset and 96.20% on the ISIC 2018 dataset, outperforming both the individual base models and several state-of-the-art CNN-based models (e.g., Gouda et al., Khan et al., Hossain et al.).
Optimizing feature extraction using a Genetic Algorithm (GA) to select 61 optimal features (23 color and 38 texture) contributed to the enhanced performance of the ensemble models.

The Max Voting method demonstrated superior precision and recall, particularly for melanoma (Class 5) and melanocytic nevi (Class 4), making it a reliable tool for distinguishing between various skin lesion types.
The study addresses limitations of current diagnostic methods, such as human error and subjectivity, by providing an automated, non-invasive, and consistent diagnostic aid.

While the model shows high accuracy, the authors acknowledge that ensemble approaches can lack interpretability, which may challenge physician confidence, and that dependence on specific datasets can limit generalization to novel clinical contexts.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on two public datasets (HAM10000 and ISIC 2018) without external validation from independent clinical centers, limiting the assessment of generalizability to diverse real-world populations.

Data augmentation was used to artificially balance class distributions (e.g., increasing underrepresented classes like dermatofibroma and vascular lesions to ~3200 images), which may not reflect the true prevalence of these lesions in clinical practice and could lead to over-optimistic performance estimates.
The paper acknowledges that ensemble models lack interpretability, which poses a significant barrier to clinical adoption as physicians may struggle to trust or understand the model's predictions.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the superiority of the Max Voting method over individual models or state-of-the-art CNNs; comparisons are based solely on descriptive metrics.

The model was trained on dermoscopic images only; its performance on non-dermoscopic clinical images (which vary in lighting, focus, and context) is unknown, limiting immediate clinical deployment relevance.
The hardware specifications (NVIDIA Titan Xp, Intel i7) are outdated relative to current standards, and computational efficiency or inference time, critical for clinical deployment, is not reported.

The comparison with state-of-the-art methods relies on reported accuracies from other studies rather than a direct, fair re-evaluation on the same test splits, potentially introducing bias in the performance comparison.
